methodology: Articles sourced from Virginia-specific RSS feeds and Google News RSS, filtered for AI/technology/workforce relevance, then assessed by Claude against the scenario framework. Each article is tagged with the region it most affects and the scenario it signals toward.

signals.title: New partnership between VCU and AI Ready RVA aims to boost AI education, innovation and community impact - VCU News
signals.signal_reading: VCU's partnership with AI Ready RVA signals intentional knowledge distribution infrastructure beyond elite tech hubs, creating peer learning networks that could spread AI fluency through Richmond's broader community rather than concentrating it in universities alone.

signals.title: ODU and Google launch AI incubator to boost innovation in Hampton Roads - 13newsnow.com
signals.signal_reading: ODU-Google incubator represents deep capability building but raises the concentration question: will this create a regional center of excellence that stays within Hampton Roads institutions, or distribute AI skills across the military-maritime workforce?

signals.title: Report says 35% of the jobs in Virginia may be at risk to artificial intelligence. Southside the least impacted, Northern Virginia the most. - Cardinal News
signals.signal_reading: Report showing Southside least impacted by AI job displacement paradoxically signals the widening gap—not because Southside is protected, but because it lacks the AI-intensive industries that drive both disruption and opportunity, reinforcing economic divergence from NoVA.

signals.title: AI pressure grows on Richmond's young workforce - Axios
signals.signal_reading: AI pressure on Richmond's young workforce suggests rapid platform-driven adoption without deep capability building—workers must adapt to AI tools deployed by employers rather than developing fundamental AI literacy that would enable them to shape the technology.

signals.title: Spanberger to consider legislation requiring state guidance on AI in schools - VPM
signals.signal_reading: Governor considering state guidance on AI in schools signals potential for distributed AI fluency starting at K-12 level, but success depends on whether guidance enables deep, hands-on learning or shallow compliance with platform adoption mandates.
